aliases: 九转大肠 / 红烧大肠

chinese: 九转大肠 / 红烧大肠

food_subgroup: Organ meats / offal — braised pork intestine

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pork large intestine food product entry; no specific entry for the Shandong braised preparation exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers pig intestine as an ingredient category. No specific braised-preparation code exists; closest parent match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for braised pork intestine dish. Left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Braised Intestines in Brown Sauce is approximately 150 g, typically presented as several braised intestine segments glazed in a rich, sweet-savory brown sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pork large intestine, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10397)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Jiu Zhuan Da Chang (braised pork intestine with sauce) exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork large intestine (NDB 10397) as the primary protein/fat/mineral anchor, with carbohydrate, sugar, and sodium adjusted upward to reflect the braising sauce (soy sauce, sugar, vinegar). Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against this USDA anchor and retained where consistent. Saturated fat estimated from USDA pork intestine fatty acid profile. Cholesterol estimated from USDA cooked pork large intestine (~262 mg/100g). All adaptations documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Jiu Zhuan Da Chang. GI estimated at 35 (low) by analogy with braised pork dishes and organ-meat preparations, which are dominated by protein and fat with minimal starch. The sauce contributes sugar (sucrose GI ~65) but at low total carbohydrate load (~8.5 g/100g). High protein and fat content substantially blunt glycemic response. GL per 150g serving = (35 × 8.5 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 4.5; rounded to 3.0 per 100g basis. Estimation basis documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (550 mg/100g) and elevated saturated fat from organ meat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (550 mg per 100g, equivalent to ~825 mg per standard 150g serving), which can significantly contribute to daily sodium limits of 2000 mg recommended by WHO; the high saturated fat and cholesterol from pork intestine further elevate cardiovascular risk in hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content with moderate glycemic index, but high saturated fat and caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low total carbohydrate content (8.5 g/100g) and estimated low GI (35) mean this dish has a modest glycemic impact, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious of the high caloric density (285 kcal/100g), elevated saturated fat (7.8 g/100g), and added sugar in the braising sauce, all of which can impair insulin sensitivity and weight management when consumed in excess.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol (~262 mg/100g), saturated fat (7.8 g/100g), and sodium (550 mg/100g) from organ meat and braising sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is high in dietary cholesterol (~393 mg per 150g serving), saturated fat, and sodium — all established risk factors for cardiovascular disease — and individuals with existing CVD or dyslipidaemia should limit consumption to occasional small portions and prioritise leaner protein sources in their regular diet.

gout.key_factor: High purine content from pork organ meat
gout.guidance: Pork large intestine is a high-purine organ meat; individuals with gout or hyperuricaemia should avoid or strictly limit this dish to prevent uric acid elevation and acute gout flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Intestines in Brown Sauce (九转大肠, Jiǔzhuǎn dàcháng), literally 'Nine-times Circulated Large Intestines', is one of the most celebrated signature dishes of Shandong (Lu) cuisine, originating in Jinan during the Qing Dynasty. The name alludes to the Taoist alchemical concept of nine-step elixir refinement, reflecting the dish's extraordinarily labour-intensive preparation. Pig's large intestines are meticulously cleaned multiple times, blanched, deep-fried until the exterior sets and colours, then slowly braised in a complex master sauce that balances five flavours simultaneously: sour (rice vinegar), sweet (rock sugar), savory (soy sauce and pork stock), spicy (white pepper and ginger), and faintly bitter (from spice reduction). The finished dish presents as glossy, deep-red braised intestine segments with a soft yet slightly springy bite and a rich, multi-layered sweet-savory glaze. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at approximately 285 kcal per 100g, with moderate protein (12.5 g), high fat (22.5 g including ~7.8 g saturated fat), and relatively low carbohydrate (8.5 g). It is a significant source of dietary cholesterol (~262 mg/100g), vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg), iron (1.8 mg), and zinc (1.5 mg). Sodium is high at 550 mg/100g due to the soy-sauce-based braising liquid. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, and cholesterol content, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its caloric density and saturated fat despite a low estimated glycemic index. It is also a high-purine food, making it unsuitable for individuals with gout. Best enjoyed as an occasional specialty dish at traditional Shandong banquets or communal dinners.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Jiu Zhuan Da Chang exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork large intestine as the primary anchor, with sauce-derived macros (carbohydrate, sugar, sodium) adjusted to reflect the braising preparation. The input AI-generated values were cross-checked against this USDA anchor and found broadly consistent. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — cooked pork large intestine (NDB 10397) as the closest approved-source anchor. Carbohydrate (8.5 g), sugar (6.5 g), and sodium (550 mg) adjusted upward from the plain cooked intestine baseline to reflect the braising sauce (soy sauce, sugar, vinegar). Saturated fat (7.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (9.2), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.4) estimated from USDA pork intestine fatty acid profile. Cholesterol (262 mg/100g) taken directly from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork large intestine. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this braised preparation is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for the sauce-braised form and cannot be reliably estimated without laboratory analysis. 3. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: pork intestine and the braising sauce ingredients do not contain industrially produced trans fats; any naturally occurring trans fat from ruminant sources is not applicable here. 4. GI estimated at 35 (low) by analogy with braised pork and organ-meat preparations per University of Sydney GI Database methodology; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. GL per 150g serving ≈ 4.5 (low). 5. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03302757 (pork large intestine food product) and FoodEx2 A0FKP (pig intestine) used as closest parent anchors; no exact braised-preparation codes exist in either system. 6. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 150g entries) — deduplicated to single 150g default serving. 7. Input source 'Shandong University' (sdu.edu.cn) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence; USDA FoodData Central substituted as the approved anchor.
